figures.gloss: The Supreme God of the Chuvash sky — remote, singular, ruling a court of subordinate spirits and approached chiefly through communal sacrifice and intermediaries; his name is the Oghur-Turkic descendant of Tengri, carried up the Volga by the Bulgars.
figures.facet: The last living branch of the Tengri word west of the steppe: a Turkic sky-father transplanted into a Finnic river landscape and worshipped there in groves and fields — steppe theology gone agrarian, the sky-god of horsemen now asked for rain on rye.
figures.provenance: V.K. Magnitsky, Materials for the Explanation of the Old Chuvash Faith (1881); N.I. Ashmarin's Chuvash dictionary; the Turӑ < Turkic *teŋri etymology is standard Turkology.

figures.gloss: The fenced sacrificial enclosure and its resident spirit: in the Chuvash legend a once-bright being of Tura's kin was slain and his scattered remains sprouted keremet sites everywhere — each village keeping its walled grove where the exacting spirit is fed lest he send sickness.
figures.facet: The shrine born of a murder: sanctity here is unquiet memory, a wronged power localised at a fenced address and kept appeased — the genius loci as an obligation the community inherits rather than a blessing it enjoys.
figures.provenance: The keremet legend and cult recorded by Magnitsky (1881) and 19th-c. Kazan ethnography; the institution and word (Arabic karāma via Volga Bulgar Islam) are shared across the Volga-Kama peoples.

figures.gloss: The communal offering — animals and ritual porridge cooked at a fixed sacred field or spring — by which the village petitions Turӑ for rain and harvest; in its great form (uchuk) whole districts gathered, and in some unbaptized Chuvash villages the rite never stopped.
figures.facet: Where the revival and the survival meet: modern Vattisen Yaly reconstitutes ceremonies that a handful of villages simply kept doing — the same rite existing simultaneously as heritage performance and as unbroken practice, the tradition's two time-streams visible in one field.
figures.provenance: Uchuk/chük described by Magnitsky (1881) and later Chuvash ethnography; surviving village practice among unbaptized Chuvash and the post-Soviet revival are documented in modern regional scholarship.

figures.gloss: The Chuvash ritual specialist — diviner, healer and finder of causes, who determines by casting and reading which spirit is offended and what offering will settle the account, and who directs the household's dealings with keremet and the dead.
figures.facet: The shamanic office gone domestic: little trance-flight remains — the yumӑҫ is a technician of diagnosis and settlement rather than a soul-traveller, showing what the steppe ecstatic specialist becomes after centuries in a sedentary village economy.
figures.provenance: The yumӑҫ is documented in Magnitsky (1881) and Ashmarin's lexicography; the contrast with full Siberian shamanism is drawn in Volga-region comparative ethnography.

figures.gloss: A named power of Turӑ's heavenly court, ranked close beneath the supreme god and associated with fate and the protection of the household; listed among the lesser deities in 19th-century Chuvash pantheon surveys alongside Pülekh and Pihampar.
figures.facet: One of the court rather than the throne: Kepe shows that Chuvash heaven, like the Volga village it mirrors, is administered — the high god delegates, and named subordinates like Kepe are where a villager's actual dealings with the sky-world happen.
figures.provenance: Listed among the Chuvash pantheon's subordinate deities in V.K. Magnitsky, Materials for the Explanation of the Old Chuvash Faith (1881); N.I. Ashmarin's Chuvash dictionary records the name and its court context.

figures.gloss: The allotter of fortune in Turӑ's court — the power who deals out each person's share of blessing, luck and lifespan on the high god's behalf; standard in 19th-century enumerations of the Chuvash pantheon.
figures.facet: The fate-office made explicit: where Turӑ is remote and general, Pülekh is the named mechanism by which that remoteness still reaches a single cradle or a single harvest — abstraction given an address.
figures.provenance: V.K. Magnitsky, Materials for the Explanation of the Old Chuvash Faith (1881); N.I. Ashmarin's Chuvash lexicography records pülĕkh as both the god's name and the common noun for 'allotted fortune.'

figures.gloss: Patron and master of wolves in the Chuvash pantheon, also credited with granting the gifts of seers and diviners; his name is a naturalization of Persian payghambar ('prophet'), carried into the pre-Islamic Chuvash pantheon by the same Volga Bulgar Islamic contact that gave the tradition keremet (< Arabic karāma).
figures.facet: A second instance of the shard's own loanword seam: an Islamic title, stripped of its original referent, resettled as the proper name of a wolf-god — proof that the boundary between 'pagan' and 'Muslim' vocabulary on the Volga was never as clean as either side later claimed.
figures.provenance: Recorded among the Chuvash pantheon's named deities by Magnitsky (1881) and Ashmarin; the payghambar etymology is standard in Volga-Bulgar/Chuvash Islamic-loanword scholarship, parallel to the keremet < karāma case already in this shard.

figures.gloss: The Chuvash winter solstice / new-year festival, centered on household luck-divination and rites for the health and increase of livestock; documented in 19th-century ethnography and still observed as a folk holiday in Chuvashia.
figures.facet: The calendar half of a religion the roster otherwise shows only in the field and the grave: Surkhuri is the turn-of-the-year counterpart to chük's harvest petition, fixing luck-seeking to the darkest point of the solar year rather than to planting or death.
figures.provenance: Described in 19th-century Chuvash ethnography (Magnitsky and successors); continuity into the modern Vattisen Yaly revival and unbaptized-village folk calendar is documented in regional post-Soviet scholarship.

figures.gloss: The Chuvash funerary and memorial complex centered on the yupa — a carved wooden post raised over or near a grave at the autumn commemorations for the dead; the practice is old enough to have named a month of the traditional Chuvash calendar, Yupa uyăhĕ ('Yupa month'), roughly November.
figures.facet: A death-rite so central it colonized the calendar itself: where most traditions mark time by feast or saint, the Chuvash folk year names a whole month after the standing wooden marker of the dead, making ancestor-memory a fixed coordinate of ordinary time rather than an occasional observance.
figures.provenance: The yupa post and its autumn commemorations, and the derived calendar month-name, are recorded in Chuvash ethnography following Magnitsky (1881) and in Ashmarin's lexicographic material on the traditional folk calendar.

edges.note: Chuvash Turӑ is the Oghur-Turkic reflex of the same sky-name as Tengri — direct linguistic descent through Volga Bulgar; the westernmost living branch of the Tengri word.

One Volga keremet institution shared across Chuvash, Mari and Udmurt villages — the word itself (Arabic karāma via Bulgar Islam) travelled with the fenced-grove cult; a documented areal kinship, though the exact direction of borrowing is debated.

Neighbouring supreme gods of one Volga-Kama religious landscape — a Turkic name and a Uralic name filling the same office of grove-worshipped high sky under the same Orthodox pressure; areal kinship, not descent.

Two rooms of the one Volga-Kama sacrificial complex — communal animal-and-porridge offering at a fixed holy place; the kinship of the institutions is plain, but specific descent between them is untraced, so tiered as analogy.

The same office of dealing with the spirits on the community's behalf, but diverged in technique: the Siberian shaman flies the three worlds in trance while the yumӑҫ diagnoses and settles accounts by divination — the ecstatic specialist domesticated.
The remote bright sky and the near dark enclosure — Turӑ petitioned for increase, keremet paid against harm; the Chuvash sacred split into a high address and a fenced one.
The same fenced-grove institution and loanword shared with the Chuvash keremet cult — completing the three-way Udmurt/Mari/Chuvash Volga-Kama keremet seam.

Functionally identical Volga-region ritual offices — the Udmurt tuno and the Chuvash yumӑҫ both diagnose which spirit is offended and prescribe the settling sacrifice — documented within the same regional comparative ethnographic literature.
Court hierarchy: Pülekh deals out Turӑ's blessings and each person's lot on the high god's behalf — the withdrawn creator's fate-office made a distinct, addressable power.
Kepe is ranked in the pantheon lists as a named member of Turӑ's heavenly court, close beneath the supreme god — the sky's administration given a face.

Both name a heaven-allotted personal fortune central to a Turkic-descended people's fate-doctrine — Pülekh as a personified distributing deity, Tengrist kut as an impersonal apportioned life-force; areal/genetic Turkic kinship of idiom, not identical offices.

Two unrelated winter-solstice festivals built around the same turn-of-year concerns — household luck, divination, and the safety of livestock — reached independently at opposite ends of the old Silk Road world.

Both fix the community's ritual year to a solstice turning-point with divinatory and luck-securing observances; a shared solstice-festival template with no claimed line of descent between Volga Chuvash and Baltic Balts.

dossier.essence: The pre-Christian village religion of the Chuvash, a Turkic-speaking people of the middle Volga descended in part from the Volga Bulgars, together with its modern revival under the name Vattisen Yaly. At its head stands Turӑ, the supreme sky-god whose name is the Oghur-Turkic descendant of Tengri; beneath him a graded world of spirits managed through communal field sacrifice and the careful appeasement of keremet powers.
dossier.cosmos: Sülti Turӑ, the high God, is remote and singular, ruling a court of subordinate spirits and approached chiefly through collective offering and intermediaries. Near at hand stand the keremet: in the legend a bright kinsman of Turӑ was slain and his scattered remains sprouted keremet sites everywhere, so every village inherits a fenced enclosure whose exacting resident must be fed lest he send sickness. Prosperity is negotiated on both fronts — increase asked of the high sky, harm bought off at the near address — and the dead remain parties to the household, remembered with food and drink at seasonal memorial rites.
dossier.practice: The central rite is the chük, the communal sacrifice of animals and ritual porridge cooked at a fixed sacred field or spring to petition Turӑ for rain and harvest; in its great district form (uchuk) whole regions once gathered. Household troubles are diagnosed and settled by the yumӑҫ, the diviner-healer who determines which spirit is offended and what offering closes the account. Most Chuvash were baptized Orthodox by the 19th century, but some unbaptized villages never stopped the rites, and post-Soviet revivalists have reconstituted them as a national tradition — survival and revival running side by side.
dossier.canon: No scripture: an oral corpus of prayers, invocations, and keremet legends, put on paper by Kazan-school ethnography — Magnitsky's 1881 'Materials' is the foundational collection, and Ashmarin's 17-volume Chuvash dictionary preserves much of the ritual vocabulary.
dossier.reflects: Religion as a two-sided offering economy — the sacred split into a high address petitioned for increase and a fenced one paid against harm, divinity kept as accounts to be settled rather than a single moral allegiance.
sources: V.K. Magnitsky, 'Materialy k ob'yasneniyu staroy chuvashskoy very' (1881)
Ahmad Ibn Fadlan, 'Ibn Fadlan's Journey to Russia', trans. Richard N. Frye (2005)
Olessia P. Vovina, 'Building the Road to the Temple: Religion and National Revival in the Chuvash Republic' (2000)

links.cite: V.K. Magnitsky, 'Materialy k ob'yasneniyu staroy chuvashskoy very' (1881)
Ahmad Ibn Fadlan, 'Ibn Fadlan's Journey to Russia', trans. Richard N. Frye (2005)
Olessia P. Vovina, 'Building the Road to the Temple: Religion and National Revival in the Chuvash Republic' (2000)
